name: jemalloc
summary: Jason Evans's multi-arena allocator with explicit dirty/muzzy page state machine and decay-based purge — the long-tail-friendly choice for long-running servers (FreeBSD libc, Firefox, Redis, Cassandra, Meta).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Jason Evans's jemalloc is a multi-arena allocator (round-robin arena assignment, default 4x CPUs) with explicit dirty/muzzy page state machine and decay-based purge. The decay-purge model — pages transitioning dirty to muzzy (`MADV_FREE`) to retained over sigmoidal decay timers — is what gives jemalloc its excellent long-tail behaviour on long-running servers.

It is the FreeBSD libc allocator and was Rust's standard allocator from 2015 to 2018; Firefox, Redis, Cassandra, and Meta's server fleet all use it.

sections.title: Decay-purge and multi-arena
sections.content:
Two-stage decay: dirty pages transit to muzzy (via `MADV_FREE`, which lets the kernel reclaim only under pressure) and finally to retained (returned to the OS). Decay timers `dirty_decay_ms` and `muzzy_decay_ms` are tunable per arena and follow a sigmoidal schedule rather than a hard cliff, which smooths reclamation work over time and keeps the allocation hot path off the decay critical path. A background thread purges with a trylock pattern so foreground allocators are never blocked on decay.

Multi-arena assignment defaults to 4x CPU count to reduce contention; a separate huge arena handles large allocations. Built-in heap profiling via `jeprof` and arena introspection via `mallctl` make jemalloc the most introspectable production allocator.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Production: FreeBSD libc, Firefox, Redis, Cassandra, Rust historically (2015 to 2018), Meta's server fleet. The decay-purge model is what gives jemalloc its excellent long-tail behaviour on long-running servers — pages that are temporarily idle return to the OS without paying eager-purge latency.
